items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.
Correct answer: Fat. “Carbohydrate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: 30 g. “10 g” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 4 kcal. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
